Title: Structural Transformation and Productivity in Asia: An Overview
Description: This paper analyzes structural transformation across Asian economies and its implications for growth. Asia has transitioned out of agriculture faster than other regions, generating substantial productivity gains through labor reallocation. Contrary to common concerns, the analysis finds no premature deindustrialization. The findings underscore the continued importance of policies that facilitate labor mobility out of agriculture to support productivity growth and enable economies to fully harness structural transformation as a driver of economic growth.
Subject: Economics

Title: Asian Development Outlook (ADO) April 2026
Description: The 2026 conflict in the Middle East has placed geopolitical tensions and energy market disruptions at the center of the global economic outlook. This report explores the implications for developing Asia and the Pacific.
Subject: Economics

Title: Your Questions Answered: How Asian Economies Can Manage Global Risks
Description: Drawing on the Asian Development Outlook (ADO) April 2026, ADB economists Matteo Lanzafame, Gabriele Ciminelli, and John Beirne discuss the region’s economic growth, and steps countries can take to manage risks.
Subject: Economics

Title: When Manufacturing Matters Most: Structural Transformation and Productivity Growth Trajectories in Developing and Emerging Economies
Description: The findings of this study suggest that low-income economies could boost productivity through strategies that combine manufacturing development with investments in institutional quality, technological infrastructure, and human capital formation. The emergence of premature deindustrialization has sparked debate over whether manufacturing-led development remains a viable strategy for low-income economies. This paper examines labor productivity growth trajectories in 31 developing and emerging economies over 1960–2019. It finds that manufacturing employment expansion provides substantial benefits for economies at lower productivity levels, indicating that targeted industrial policies can serve as powerful tools for catch-up growth. However, realizing these benefits requires comprehensive approaches that address multiple structural constraints simultaneously.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Low-Cost Low-Touch Information Provision, Parental Involvement, and Student Learning Outcomes: Evidence from a Government-Implemented Intervention in Indonesia
Description: [The Journal of Development Studies] Parental involvement in children’s education is often constrained by limited information, low agency, and weak incentives. This study examines whether a government-led education system can address these barriers through a low-cost, low-touch intervention implemented by public schools and teachers in Kebumen, Indonesia, where teacher capacity is moderate but learning outcomes have stagnated. The intervention comprised three components: teachers provided regular updates on students’ learning progress; parents could write responses directly to teachers through a feedback section; and monthly leaflets offered simple, practical ways to support learning at home. The intervention was implemented entirely through existing school structures at a cost less than USD 1 per student over 14 months. Our randomised results show greater parental engagement and communication with teachers, and higher teacher motivation and support for students. Ten months later, mathematics scores improved among lower-grade students, while reading motivation declined among higher-grade students. A follow-up qualitative study indicates that parents’ limited ability to assist with learning and insufficient teacher support during pandemic-induced school closures constrained progress, particularly for older students.
Subject: Education;Social development and protection

Title: Data Innovations in Armenia, Georgia, Kazakhstan, Thailand, and Viet Nam - Delivering Data Impact in Asia and the Pacific, Volume 1, 2026
Description:
This publication outlines how ADB is helping countries use innovative technologies such as mobile phone data and satellite imagery to improve the collection of the high-quality, timely, and seasonally adjusted data needed to bolster policymaking.
Focused on Armenia, Georgia, Kazakhstan, Thailand, and Viet Nam, it assesses the shift towards digital economies and highlights projects using GPS data to track displacement during disasters, air pollution alert apps, and crop monitoring technologies. It shows how blending traditional sampling with robust data and tools such as machine learning can help countries strengthen evidence-based decision-making, better tackle challenges, and drive sustainable economic growth.
Subject: Agriculture and natural resources;Capacity development;Economics;ICT;Knowledge management

Title: Monthly Debt Roundup (March 2026)
Description: The ABO Monthly Debt Roundup provides a snapshot on changes in financial prices such as foreign exchange, yields, equity as well as recent monetary policy rate movements in the region. In addition, the ABO Monthly Debt Roundup provides data on foreign investor bond flows of local currency government bonds for the PRC, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines and Thailand.
Subject: Capacity development;Finance;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Markets Aren’t Neutral—Why Competition Policy Needs to Pay Attention to Women
Description: Competition policy focuses on prices and efficiency but often misses how mergers or market changes affect women and men differently. Competition policy is often described as gender-neutral: markets respond to incentives, not identities. But markets don’t operate in vacuums. They are shaped by long-standing gaps between women and men in access to jobs, capital, and economic opportunities. Understanding how market rules interact with inequality is essential.
Subject: Economics;Gender;Industry and trade

Title: Weekly Debt Highlights (March 2026)
Description: [March 2,, 16, 23, and 30, 2026] The Weekly Debt Highlights Report provides a summary of the relevant key macroeconomic data and monetary policy updates released in the previous week in the various economies covered by ABO. The report also includes select government and corporate debt issuances, credit rating updates, and other relevant regulatory developments. The report is published every Monday.
Subject: Capacity development;Finance;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Asia Bond Monitor - March 2026
Description: [Emerging East Asian Local Currency Bond Markets: A Regional Update] Emerging East Asian financial markets remained resilient between 3 November 2025 and 6 February 2026 despite rising global uncertainties, according to the Asia Bond Monitor March 2026. Strong fourth quarter (Q4) growth and renewed optimism around AI-related supply chains helped offset heightened geopolitical risks and AI valuation concerns. The region recorded equity gains, net portfolio inflows, and currency appreciation against the US dollar. Risk premiums diverged across markets, while bond yields broadly tracked movements in advanced economies. The region's local currency bond outstanding reached USD30.6 trillion by the end of 2025, with issuance moderating to USD2.9 trillion in Q4. ASEAN+3's sustainable bond outstanding totaled USD1.0 trillion by end-2025, supported by robust issuance of USD275.9 billion during the year. ASEAN+3 led all global regions in sustainable bond issuance in 2025.
Subject: Economics;Finance;Industry and trade

Title: The 2026 Conflict in the Middle East and Macroeconomic Risks for Asia and the Pacific
Description: This brief examines how the 2026 conflict in the Middle East affects Asia and the Pacific, estimating impacts on economic growth and inflation in various scenarios. It recommends policies focus on macroeconomic stability, reduced energy consumption, and energy diversification.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: It Is About Time: Measurement, Challenges, and Policies for Asia
Description: This report explores how time-use surveys can provide insights into the paid and unpaid activities that sustain economies and societies, highlighting the importance of investing in care, infrastructure, and labor market reforms.
Subject: Economics;Gender

Title: Does tourism inflow help merchandise exports of Pacific Island economies? A gravity analysis
Description: [Asian Economic Journal] This paper empirically analyzes tourist inflows as a determinant of merchandise exports for the Pacific countries by employing a gravity model technique. Other relevant variables that augment the model are trade agreements, language affinity, and visa policies. The paper finds that inbound tourism to Pacific countries significantly increases merchandise exports: 1% increase in tourist inflows is associated with a 0.17% rise in merchandise exports in the region. This relationship is along expected lines, as international tourist arrivals can help reduce trade costs, increasing such exports. If international tourists come from countries that share a common language and have established economic partnerships through FTAs and relaxed visa policies, the increase in tourist arrivals is notably bigger.
Subject: Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Work from Home and Fertility
Description: This study examines how realized and planned fertility are associated with work from home (WFH) arrangements. Using harmonized surveys across 38 economies and more than 100,000 responses in the United States, it documents that adults who WFH at least one day per week report more recent births and larger intended family size than comparable non-WFH peers. When both partners WFH, total lifetime fertility is higher by about 0.2 children. Findings are weaker in Asia, where WFH is less common.
Subject: Economics;Gender

Title: Fostering High-Impact Entrepreneurship in Regional Clusters
Description: This policy brief examines the hypothesis that the geographic concentration of entrepreneurial activity in regional hotspots is positively correlated with the quality of that activity. It argues that while start-up formation is widespread, the conditions necessary for scaling—deep knowledge networks, sophisticated investors, specialized human capital, and supportive institutions—coalesce in only a limited number of places. Therefore, sustained competitiveness depends not on increasing start-up counts, but on building ecosystem depth that enables start-ups to scale.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Asian Development Review 2026: Volume 43, Number 1
Description: The latest issue covers diverse development and policy themes across Asia. Several studies examine how trade liberalization, market competition, and technology imports shape employment structures and productivity in Viet Nam and India. Urban development is explored through new evidence on the property value effects of Jakarta’s mass transit investments. Other contributions investigate the role of demolition compensation in fostering entrepreneurship, use machine learning to analyze financial sector linkages with the real economy in Malaysia, and study interregional fund flows in Cambodia’s dollarized system. The issue also features analyses of piped water access and food consumption, poverty and food insecurity dynamics in Pakistan, and the regional adoption of debt for nature swaps.
Subject: Economics

Title: Beyond Pretty Pictures: Combined Single- and Multi-Image Super-Resolution for Sentinel-2 Images
Description: [IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing] Super-resolution aims to increase the resolution of satellite images by reconstructing high-frequency details, which go beyond naïve upsampling. This has particular relevance for Earth observation missions like Sentinel-2, which offer frequent, regular coverage at no cost; but at coarse resolution. Its pixel footprint is too large to capture small features like houses, streets, or hedge rows. To address this, we present SEN4X, a hybrid super-resolution architecture that combines the advantages of single-image and multi-image techniques. It combines temporal oversampling from repeated Sentinel-2 acquisitions with a learned prior from high-resolution Pléiades Neo data. In doing so, SEN4X upgrades Sentinel-2 imagery to 2.5 m ground sampling distance. We test the super-resolved images on urban land-cover classification in Hanoi, Vietnam. We find that they lead to a significant performance improvement over state-of-the-art super-resolution baselines.
Subject: Economics;Urban Development

Title: How Will the Conflict in the Middle East Affect Economies in Asia and the Pacific?
Description: Surging fuel prices and disruption to shipping risk widespread fallout across the region, but policymakers should resist market intervention in favor of support measures for vulnerable groups.
Subject: Economics;Energy

Title: Bridging the gender data gap: measuring welfare losses from climate-related disasters for men and women
Description: [Natural Hazards] This study addresses the gender data gap in disaster contexts, a major barrier to achieving the Sustainable Development Goal of equitable climate action. Using a novel rapid assessment survey tool, we collected sex disaggregated data from 2,031 individuals across 37 coastal villages in Bangladesh, one week after Cyclone Remal in May 2024. The analysis extends beyond the conventional comparison of male- and female-headed households by capturing individual-level welfare losses for men and women. It uses a comprehensive, multi-domain welfare framework encompassing food security, sanitation, healthcare, paid and unpaid work, and gender-based violence. Findings reveal widespread declines in food security, sanitation, healthcare, and paid employment for both men and women. Women faced greater barriers to healthcare access, while men experienced more severe sanitation challenges. Our findings also reveal that women spent significantly more time collecting water and cooking fuel due to disrupted infrastructure and gendered domestic roles. Interruptions in paid employment primarily drove men’s income losses. Intersectional analysis indicates that less educated women suffer higher food insecurity than men with comparable education levels. In areas experiencing severe damage, reports of gender-based violence also rose. The study demonstrates that rapid, individual-level, sex-disaggregated data can be collected in the immediate aftermath of disasters, providing an empirically grounded basis for equitable response and recovery policies and inclusive climate resilience planning.
Subject: Disaster risk management;Economics;Gender

Title: Impact of the 2025 United States Tariffs on Firm Export Behavior: Evidence from Asian Customs Data
Description: This paper dissects the impact of United States (US) tariffs introduced in 2025 on firm exports by using comprehensive export customs data to the US from six selected Asian countries, concentrating on heterogeneity by firm size and product type for analyses. Estimation results suggest that tariffs substantially compressed margins among micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises over the short run in order to maintain US export levels.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Asian Economic Integration Report 2026: Leveraging Regional Cooperation and Integration to Navigate Global Uncertainties
Description:
This report highlights that Asia and the Pacific is continuing to deepen economic integration amid global uncertainties. It sets out the latest trends and discusses policy priorities to help strengthen resilience and advance inclusive, sustainable integration.
 
The report notes that trade remains the strongest driver of integration, with economies adapting to shifting policies and supply chains. Intraregional investment is resilient, supported by rapid digital growth and rising opportunities from artificial intelligence. Financial integration progresses slowly, highlighting the need for stronger infrastructure, better risk management, and improved cross border payments. Remittances and tourism remain robust, though high skilled migration is concentrated outside the region.
Subject: Capacity development;Economics;Finance;ICT;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Weekly Debt Highlights (February 2026)
Description: [February 2, 9, 16, and 23, 2026] The Weekly Debt Highlights Report provides a summary of the relevant key macroeconomic data and monetary policy updates released in the previous week in the various economies covered by ABO. The report also includes select government and corporate debt issuances, credit rating updates, and other relevant regulatory developments. The report is published every Monday.
Subject: Capacity development;Finance;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Examining Container Terminal Efficiency with Diverse Data Sources: Vessel, Truck, and Container Turnaround Times in Japanese Terminals
Description: [Logistics] Improving container terminal efficiency requires a comprehensive understanding of the interactions between vessel, truck, and container operations, yet existing studies often analyzed these components separately. In Japanese container terminals, where digitalization initiatives are progressing, empirical evidence based on integrated operational data remains limited. Methods: This study empirically analyzes turnaround times for vessels, trucks, and containers at five major Japanese container terminals using a composite dataset that integrates terminal operating system data, automatic identification system data, and liner service information. Descriptive statistical analyses and regression models are applied to examine vessel berthing time, truck arrival patterns and turnaround time, container dwell time within terminals, and container round-trip time outside terminals. Results: The analysis reveals distinct temporal patterns in terminal operations, including systematic morning–afternoon asymmetries and differences across cargo flows. Truck turnaround times increase with vessel calls and vary by time of day, while container dwell times are strongly influenced by terminal policies such as free-time rules. Regression analyses indicate that turnaround times are primarily affected by terminal-controlled factors. Conclusions: These findings demonstrate the importance of synchronizing quayside and landside operations. The study contributes integrated empirical evidence to the port digitalization literature and provides actionable insights for enhancing container terminal efficiency.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: What Did They Say? Respondent Identity, Question Framing and the Measurement of Employment
Description: [The World Bank Economic Review] Using data from a primary survey conducted in rural India, this paper examines how two key survey design features—respondent identity and question framing—affect employment estimates. First, it estimates the causal impact of (a) replacing a single weekly employment question with a set of detailed activity-specific questions, and (b) changing the reference period from a week to individual days. The detailed module yields significantly higher estimates of women’s employment with no corresponding effect for men. Second, using spousal respondent pairs, the paper finds that proxy-reports by men significantly underestimate women’s employment while men's employment estimates do not differ between self- and proxy- reports. Within different types of employment however there are significant deviations for both genders. Intra-household analysis suggests misreporting is driven by asymmetric information and gender norms. Overall, the findings underscore the importance of self-reporting and detailed questions for accurately measuring employment with implications for improving survey design in resource-constrained contexts.
Subject: Economics;Social development and protection

Title: Fiscal and monetary policies for inclusive growth in developing and emerging markets [Editorial]
Description: [Journal of International Money and Finance] This special issue of the Journal of International Money and Finance (JIMF) features selected research papers that were presented at a conference jointly organized by the Asian Development Bank and the Bank of Korea in Seoul on 16–17 July 2025.1 The theme of the conference was “Fiscal and Monetary Policies for Inclusive Growth in Developing and Emerging Markets”. The conference was set against the context of persistent inequality and economic disparities, which augments the role of fiscal and monetary policies in fostering inclusive growth. Developing and emerging markets face unique challenges and opportunities in crafting policies that not only stimulate economic growth but also ensure that the benefits are equitably distributed. The papers in the special issue, which have gone through the regular refereeing process for the journal, provide new insights on how emerging markets can leverage fiscal and monetary policies to achieve more inclusive growth outcomes.
Subject: Economics;Governance and public sector management

Title: Impact of climate vulnerability on fiscal risk: Do religious tensions and financial development matter?
Description: [Energy Economics] Using data from around 100 countries between 1995 and 2019, we examine the impact of climate vulnerability on fiscal risk. We also explore the transmission channels that are important for the relationship between climate vulnerability and fiscal risk. Our results show that in highly climate-vulnerable economies, increased vulnerability leads to higher government bond yields by 0.5 to 1.5 percentage point and lower sovereign debt ratings by up to one notch over two years. These effects are exacerbated by religious tensions as a form of political instability but mitigated by well-developed financial markets. Therefore, even though fiscal consolidation is crucial for containing fiscal risks in the case of climate vulnerability, political stability and financial development also matter.
Subject: Economics

Title: Achieving the Golden Indonesia Vision 2045: Pathways and Challenges
Description: This book provides a comprehensive perspective on Indonesia’s Golden Vision 2045. Analyzing obstacles and challenges, it shows how Indonesia can transform itself into a resilient, inclusive, prosperous country by its centenary year.
Subject: Economics

Title: Private Sector Development and Policy Actions in Timor-Leste: Lessons from a Business Mapping Survey
Description: This paper empirically investigates what drives the growth of private businesses in Timor-Leste and the policy actions needed to facilitate growth amid global uncertainty, especially for micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs). Estimation results show that four types of MSMEs—those formalized, digitalized, internationalized, and women-led—exhibit different business characteristics and performance results compared with control groups. Accordingly, policy support areas differ by firm type, with a focused approach critical in designing and delivering policy assistance to MSMEs in Timor-Leste.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Workers’ Exposure to Artificial Intelligence Across Development Stages
Description: This paper finds that workers in low-income countries face lower exposure to artificial intelligence than those in high-income countries, mainly due to within-occupation task differences, and discusses the implications. The study develops a country-specific measure of workers’ exposure to artificial intelligence that accounts for variation in occupational tasks across development contexts. It finds that information and communications technology intensity and human capital explain most variation. High-income countries employ more workers in highly exposed occupations, with rising exposure driven by task shifts. The findings suggest that existing disparities in ICT infrastructure and digital readiness could widen the technological and economic divide between advanced and emerging economies.
Subject: Economics;ICT;Social development and protection

Title: Heat Stress, Air Pollution Risk and Population Exposure: Evidence from Selected Asian Countries
Description: This paper examines heat stress and air pollution in Bangladesh, Indonesia, Pakistan, Thailand, and Viet Nam, looking at patterns of exposure among different population groups and discussing policy implications. The findings reveal country-specific patterns in the correlation between heat stress and air pollution risk and in population exposure across demographic profiles. The results suggest the importance of targeted policies to mitigate the compounded effects of extreme temperatures and air pollution on vulnerable populations.
Subject: Climate change;Health

Title: Weekly Debt Highlights (January 2026)
Description: [January 12, 19, and 26, 2025] The Weekly Debt Highlights Report provides a summary of the relevant key macroeconomic data and monetary policy updates released in the previous week in the various economies covered by ABO. The report also includes select government and corporate debt issuances, credit rating updates, and other relevant regulatory developments. The report is published every Monday.
Subject: Capacity development;Finance;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Climate Shocks and Noncommunicable Diseases Among Older People in India
Description: This paper shares findings from a study of the impact of temperature and rainfall on the incidence of noncommunicable diseases among older people in India. The study uses estimates from the Indian Demographic and Health Surveys from 2019 to 2021 linked with georeferenced recent meteorological data. It finds that unexpected excess heat has significant impacts on the prevalence of hypertension, high blood glucose levels, and overweight or obese status. The findings underscore the importance of policies promoting climate resilience for older people in developing economies.
Subject: Climate change;Health

Title: Public spending, private gains: the gendered impact of exogenous fiscal policy shocks
Description: [Journal of International Money and Finance] This paper examines the gender-specific effects of exogenous public spending shocks across a global sample, using a novel identification strategy and local projections. We distinguish between investment and consumption shocks and further decompose spending by function—education, health, and social protection. Public investment shocks generally reduce female labor force participation but increase wage shares and lower maternal mortality. Consumption shocks also lower participation but raise service-sector employment and tertiary enrolment in the short term. Functionally, education spending delays labor force entry but improves wages and enrolment; health spending boosts agricultural employment but may initially increase maternal mortality; and social protection stabilizes rural employment while reducing overall participation. Nonlinear analyses reveal strong heterogeneity by income level, initial gender inequality, and labor informality. For example, investment boosts female employment in poorer EMDEs but reinforces participation gaps in richer ones. These results highlight the need for gender-responsive fiscal frameworks tailored to structural conditions, and show that fiscal design—not just scale—shapes inclusive development outcomes.
Subject: Economics

Title: Youth Entrepreneurship in Bhutan: Building Pathways to Job Creation
Description: Analyzing why Bhutan faces a youth unemployment challenge as its economy diversifies, this report explores how supporting entrepreneurship could unlock productivity and drive resilient economic growth.
Subject: Economics

Title: Long-Term Outcomes of Multi-Context Childhood Poverty: Evidence from Long Panel Data from Indonesia
Description: This paper uses data from Indonesia to examine the long-term outcomes associated with multiple types of childhood deprivation and highlights policy implications. The paper constructs a measure of poverty that considers four areas—family, community, economic, and social. It then uses individual-level data spanning 21 years to examine the long-term outcomes associated with these types of childhood deprivation. It finds that adverse outcomes in adulthood are robustly associated not only with a poor family but also with a poor community. It also finds that both economic and social domains matter, though the economic domain’s influence is stronger.
Subject: Poverty;Social development and protection

Title: Public spending and inclusive growth: A cross-country empirical analysis
Description: [Journal of International Money and Finance] We investigate the effects of different components of government spending on inclusive growth. More specifically, we consider the inclusive impact of public spending on environmental protection, health, education, housing, and social protection, all of which can conceivably promote inclusive growth. For our empirical analysis, we apply panel regressions and local projections to a comprehensive database of 191 countries between 1980 and 2023. Our evidence indicates that equity-promoting government spending reduces income inequality, as measured by the Gini index, and improves human development indicators. Moreover, our analysis reveals that poorer households benefit disproportionately, suggesting that targeted fiscal expenditures can promote equity. Notably, the inclusive effects are most pronounced in advanced economies, where robust fiscal frameworks support and amplify such effects. In contrast, emerging and developing economies experience more modest gains. Overall, the findings highlight the importance of well-designed public spending programs for equitable growth. Finally, we conduct state-dependent local projections and a regional sub-sample analysis.
Subject: Economics;Finance

Title: Misinformation belief, health behavior, and labor supply during the COVID-19 pandemic: Evidence from tricycle drivers in the Philippines
Description: [Journal of Behavioral and Experimental Economics] This study aims to analyze the behavioral consequences of people’s beliefs in conspiracy theories and misinformation surrounding COVID-19 vaccines. We employ unique panel data to examine the relationship among belief in misinformation, vaccination behavior, and labor supply of tricycle drivers in the Philippines. We find that individuals with higher risk preference are more likely to hold misinformed beliefs. These beliefs, in turn, are associated with reductions in vaccination and other preventive health behaviors. We also find that beliefs in misinformation delay workplace recovery.
Subject: Economics;Health;Social development and protection;Transport

Title: Geopolitical risk, capital flow volatility, and asset market spillovers
Description: [Pacific-Basin Finance Journal] Geopolitical shocks are found to heighten financial uncertainty and disrupt cross-border capital movements, with the magnitude of these effects depending on domestic structural characteristics. This paper examines the effects of country-specific geopolitical risk on capital flow volatility and asset markets across 29 emerging and advanced economies over the period 2000–2023. Using panel regressions and a panel structural VAR framework, the results show that geopolitical risk raises bond yields and leads to exchange rate depreciation, with stronger and more persistent effects in emerging economies. Asset markets for advanced economies are affected mainly through lower equity prices. The impact on capital flow volatility is slightly higher on average for advanced economies but remains more persistent for emerging economies. Greater financial development, higher central bank independence, and lower public debt mitigate the adverse effects of geopolitical risk on both capital flows and asset markets. These findings highlight the importance of strong macroeconomic fundamentals and institutional frameworks in building resilience against geopolitical shocks.
Subject: Economics

Title: Oil price pass-through to inflation in emerging Asia
Description:
[Journal of Asian Economics] Volume 103, March 2026, 102103
 This paper provides estimates of oil price pass-through (OPPT) to both producer and consumer prices for nine emerging Asian economies using a time-varying parameter SVAR model over the period 1991–2023. We further examine how global factors affect the transmission of oil prices to producer and consumer prices, specifically via shocks in global output, US monetary policy, and global financial market uncertainty. Overall, we find that OPPT is less than proportionate and mostly higher for OPPT to producer than consumer prices, while pass-through estimates also tend to be higher in the long term. In addition, we find that OPPT has been declining for most Asian EMEs in the period after the global financial crisis of 2008. Finally, while the responsiveness of OPPT to global shocks varies depending on the type of shock, contractionary US monetary policy shocks overall most significantly amplify OPPT for both producer and consumer prices.
Subject: Economics

Title: Predicting Road Quality and Accessibility Indicators through Conventional and Innovative Methods
Description:
Road quality monitoring plays a crucial role in ensuring safe, reliable, and efficient transportation systems. These, in turn, drive economic development, promote social inclusion, and improve access to essential services. By regularly assessing road conditions, governments and agencies can 
make informed, data-driven decisions to prioritize maintenance, allocate resources effectively, and prevent costly deterioration. Such monitoring also contributes to disaster resilience and ensures that rural and underserved communities remain connected. These are key components of equitable and 
sustainable development.
This JFPR-supported publication provides an overview of different approaches to monitoring road quality. It serves as a supplement to the Guidebook on Machine Learning Techniques for Road Quality Monitoring published in March 2025. The guidebook offers a comprehensive guide to implementing 
machine learning methods. This technical study focuses on the rationale and introduces core concepts related to monitoring road quality and its corresponding metrics. In particular, it includes a review of traditional methods for collecting road quality data, and a discussion on innovative 
technologies, such as using geospatial data and machine learning techniques. These can be further explored for more granular and timely data collection.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade;Transport

Title: Harnessing Survey Data to Shape the Future of Work: A Descriptive Statistical Analysis of Jobs, Skills, and New Forms of Employment in Bhutan, Georgia, and the Philippines
Description:
Understanding how workers perform tasks, use skills, and adapt to technological change is essential for designing policies that promote inclusive growth and decent work. Across Asia and the Pacific, economies continue to experience rapid structural and technological transformation, reshaping labor 
markets and altering the nature of jobs. Yet comparable data that capture how work is changing remain limited, constraining efforts to accurately inform more-targeted labor and skills policies.
The Jobs and Skills Survey (JSS) module was developed to help fill this gap. By integrating a skills and task-based approach into national labor force surveys, the JSS module measures how workers perform their jobs, how they use digital and cognitive skills, and how they adjust to new technologies 
and work arrangements. The module provides deeper insights into topics such as job satisfaction and quality, skills development and employer demand, and role or task displacement due to technological innovation. Such information helps strengthen the foundation for policies that support decent work, inclusive growth, and resilient labor markets.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Weekly Debt Highlights (December 2025)
Description: [December 2, 8, 15, and 22, 2025] The Weekly Debt Highlights Report provides a summary of the relevant key macroeconomic data and monetary policy updates released in the previous week in the various economies covered by ABO. The report also includes select government and corporate debt issuances, credit rating updates, and other relevant regulatory developments. The report is published every Monday.
Subject: Capacity development;Finance;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Policy Approaches to Support Green Financing of Micro, Small, and Medium-Sized Enterprises: Asia-Pacific Financial Inclusion Forum
Description: This report draws on the experience of Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation members to highlight ways of improving much-needed green financing for micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) by increasing capacity, harnessing technology, and building knowledge.
Subject: Finance;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Practical Guide on Seasonal Adjustment Using JDemetra+
Description:
This guide presents JDemetra+, an advanced application developed in accordance with international best practices to facilitate transparent and high-quality seasonal adjustment processes that adhere to globally recognized standards.
Seasonal adjustment effectively eliminates systematic and recurring fluctuations that may obscure genuine movements within data, thereby unveiling the fundamental economic trends in infra-annual statistics. Through the integration of the esteemed X-13ARIMA-SEATS and TRAMO/SEATS methodologies, JDemetra+ ensures a unified and reliable framework for generating results that are internationally comparable. JDemetra+ promotes rigorous and reproducible workflows, thereby reinforcing the credibility and clarity of reports concerning critical economic indicators. JDemetra+ is essential for accurate interpretation of short-term economic indicators.
Subject: Capacity development;Economics;ICT

Title: Mapping the Spatial Distribution of Poverty in Maldives
Description:
This report analyzes the potential benefits and challenges of machine learning and small area estimation (SAE) to monitor poverty and help tackle inequality in Maldives.
It explains how SAE can generate granular poverty estimates using household survey and census data, and how machine learning including convolutional neural networks were developed using a model trained on Indonesian data. The report highlights inconsistencies and challenges such as limited sample sizes and shows how developing localized models and synchronizing data collection could help Maldives improve its poverty mapping to drive equitable and targeted interventions.
Subject: Economics;ICT;Poverty

Title: QGIS for Agricultural Statistics: A Practical Guide
Description:
Geographic information systems (GIS) have become critical for the use of agricultural statistics, especially in government analysis and planning.
This handbook offers step-by-step training on the application of the open-space platform QGIS to agricultural statistics. Across its eight modules, readers begin with foundational QGIS concepts, and then gradually develop their skills—from basic mapping and data management to advanced spatial analysis. The handbook is designed for researchers, economists, and other professionals working in agricultural statistics who are seeking practical GIS skills. No prior GIS background is required – only the desire to support more effective agricultural policymaking.
Subject: Capacity development;Economics;Knowledge management

Title: Mapping the Spatial Distribution of Poverty Using Satellite Imagery and Other Geospatial Data in Indonesia
Description:
This report shows how Indonesia can combine satellite imagery and geospatial data with machine learning to create maps showing areas of poverty, which could help shape targeted support for vulnerable communities.
Based on a collaborative study with Statistics Indonesia (BPS) and World Data Lab, the report shows how Indonesia can develop a grid-like poverty map. Computer vision and machine learning can help identify roads, buildings, and vegetation, and that information can be harnessed to estimate poverty levels. The report details poverty reduction efforts in the diverse archipelago and shows how geospatial data can help improve social protection and resource allocation.
Subject: Economics;Environment;ICT;Poverty

Title: Accelerating Economic Growth: Malaysia’s Transition Toward a High-Income Economy
Description:
This publication is part of a collection of studies that examine the experiences of Indonesia, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and Kazakhstan in global value chains.
The report demonstrates how global value chains can serve as a lens for understanding structural transformation, economic diversification, and policy choices needed to sustain inclusive growth in diverse development contexts. It is designed as a technical reference for analysts and policymakers to study the advancement of Malaysia’s economy through its participation in globalized production processes. It also aims to assess where Malaysia stands within today’s global production networks, identify where and how it can move up the value chain, and consider what policy actions are needed to turn opportunities into reality—one that builds deeper linkages between foreign investors and local firms, fosters innovation and skills, and positions Malaysia to thrive in a more fragmented, competitive, and sustainability-conscious global economy.
Subject: Capacity development;Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Enhancing Global Comparability—Asia and the Pacific in the International Comparison Program: A Special Supplement of Key Indicators for Asia and the Pacific 2025
Description:
This report analyzes living standards and price levels in Asia and the Pacific in the context of the global economy. It uses indicators based on the purchasing power parities (PPP) produced by the International Comparison Program (ICP).
The report highlights that Asia and the Pacific has become the main engine of global economic growth and discusses the importance of PPP-based indicators for intereconomy comparisons for policymaking and research. It marks 20 years of the Asian Development Bank’s role as the regional implementing agency for the ICP in Asia and the Pacific. It outlines refinements in methodology and notes how producing these statistics of regional and global public value has strengthened capacity and enhanced collaboration among participating economies.
Subject: Capacity development;Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Mobilizing Capital for Local Government Finance in Asia Through Green, Social, and Sustainability Bond Markets
Description:
"The report aims to advocate for the role of local governments in mobilizing capital—particularly through the issuance of green, social, sustainable, and other labeled bonds—and in channeling
these resources toward sustainable municipal infrastructure development and socially inclusive growth."
Subject: Finance

Title: Developing a Data Integration Tool for Poverty and Vulnerability Assessment: Reflections from a Pilot Initiative in the Philippines
Description:
In many developing economies, there is a need to improve inclusive urban planning, disaster risk reduction, and poverty and vulnerability mapping by integrating advanced technology and data analytics.
Combining Global Positioning System data, census data, satellite imagery, and survey responses into a user-friendly platform, a data integration tool called Poverty Impact and Vulnerability Evaluation or PIVE was pilot-tested in select areas in the Philippines, with the purpose of supporting public sector decision-making for optimized public transportation routes, and identifying the needs of vulnerable communities.
The PIVE tool is also intended to strengthen disaster risk reduction efforts by integrating hazard maps with settlement data and to promote inclusivity through community engagement tools.
Subject: Capacity development;Disaster risk management;Economics

Title: Financing Southeast Asia’s Shift to Cleaner Energy
Description: The blog highlights Southeast Asia’s urgent need to transition to cleaner energy systems. It explains how heavy reliance on imported fossil fuels and high financing costs hinder renewable energy adoption. The post calls for lower financing costs, stronger policies, and regional cooperation to make energy cleaner, more reliable, and affordable.
Subject: Finance

Title: Long-Term Effects of Group-Based Agricultural Support Amid COVID-19 Shocks in Nepal
Description: This paper assesses impacts of agricultural support in western Nepal and finds that it strengthened farmers’ resilience to COVID-19 disruptions. From 2014 to 2022, farmers received varying combinations of training, input subsidies, assets, and credit, channeled through agricultural groups. The study analyzed household panel data from 2014, 2018, and 2022 to estimate effects. The analysis indicates that support significantly raised crop productivity, value, sales, and income, especially support that included training, assets, or credit. Assistance after 2018 offset pandemic shocks, while earlier support improved lasting resilience. Effects of support in both periods appear generally progressive with respect to income.
Subject: Agriculture and natural resources;Economics

Title: Incentivizing Inclusion: Experimental Evidence on Lending to Women-Led Firms in Viet Nam
Description: This paper shares findings from a study on incentive designs for bank lending agents in Viet Nam that aimed to increase lending to firms led by women. It shows how well-designed incentives can improve financial inclusion while maintaining overall portfolio performance. Through a randomized controlled trial implemented with a commercial bank, the study compared contest-style and piece-rate rewards. The incentives significantly increased women-focused lending without trade-offs in terms of other business or loan quality. The contest had larger effects, especially on the performance of female lending agents.
Subject: Economics;Gender;Industry and trade

Title: Asian Development Outlook (ADO) December 2025
Description: Stronger-than-expected exports and reduced trade uncertainty have contributed to raised growth forecasts for economies in developing Asia and the Pacific for 2025 and 2026.
Subject: Capacity development;Economics;Governance and public sector management

Title: Climate Laws and Green Finance: The Value of Legal Commitment
Description: This paper provides the first empirical evidence that legally binding climate commitments mobilize sustainable finance. Using a staggered difference-in-differences design, it finds that adopting net-zero climate laws raises the share of environmental, social, and governance fund assets by about 5 percentage points within five years. The effect operates through tighter mitigation policies and lower policy uncertainty. These findings highlight that credible legal commitments can anchor expectations and accelerate the flow of capital toward the climate transition.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Tourism and Export Earnings in Southeast Asia
Description: [Journal of Southeast Asian Economies] Tourism is a significant sector in many Southeast Asian economies, with tourism exports making up a high share of total services exports in countries such as Cambodia, Lao People’s Democratic Republic, and Thailand. This article aims to improve understanding of the role international tourism plays in export earnings in Southeast Asia. The article explores the sector’s linkage to merchandise trade and examines multilateral, regional and unilateral policies to safeguard tourism services trade in the region. The article concludes that a 1 per cent in international tourist inflows to Southeast Asia is associated with a 0.62% increase in the region’s merchandise exports. Looking at services trade commitments for the tourism sector, the article finds that liberalization commitments under ASEAN’s own ASEAN Framework Agreement on Services are the most advanced. The countries did not go beyond their multilateral General Agreement on Trade in Services commitments under the megaregional Regional Comprehensive Economic Partnership. Further, as COVID-19 waned, the recovery of inbound tourism depended largely on countries’ unilateral policies to support the sector. That said, countries in Southeast Asia need to recognize the value of the linkage between inbound tourism and their merchandise exports, and develop unilateral and regional policies to serve their economic interests accordingly.
Subject: Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Learning at the Last Mile: Evidence from a Randomized Controlled Trial of Computer-Assisted Instruction in Remote Schools of the Philippines
Description: The paper presents a randomized trial in 42 remote Philippine schools that tested tablet-based, computer-assisted instruction as schools transitioned from COVID-19 closures to face-to-face instruction. Junior high school students were provided tablets with digitized learning modules that connected to school servers via local area networks. The intervention improved learning in mathematics, but not English, with intent-to-treat effects of 0.34 and treatment-on-treated effects of 0.46 standard deviations. Effects were greater for students with higher baseline “grit” and achievement. The small subset of schools that continued using the materials for a second year achieved effects of 1.6 standard deviations.
Subject: Economics;Education

Title: Nurturing Nutrition: Evidence from a Randomized Trial of Structured Electronic Food Vouchers in the Philippines
Description: The paper describes results from a rigorous pilot, which tested structured electronic food vouchers for poor households, so as to inform the design of a national voucher program in the Philippines. The pilot intervention consisted of monthly vouchers structured into food group allocations plus nutrition education, tested via a matched pair cluster-randomized trial involving 4,883 repeatedly surveyed households. Vouchers significantly increased food spending, dietary diversity, and food security, though dietary and hunger effect magnitudes were modest. Impacts were stronger for proteins and fruits and vegetables, and in urban areas, smaller households, soon after redemptions, among those with greater nutrition knowledge, and among conditional cash transfer recipients. Corresponding program adjustments could enhance effectiveness.
Subject: Economics;Poverty;Social development and protection

Title: Enhancing Productive Entrepreneurship Through Institutional Framework Conditions
Description: This brief presents an evidence-based assessment of how institutional framework conditions (IFCs) shape productive entrepreneurship across economic contexts. It describes how institutional improvements can enhance entrepreneurial productivity, especially in developing economies. IFCs such as business regulations, the rule of law, and financial development play a central role in shaping entrepreneurial behavior. The brief focuses on these IFCs and examines how their effects differ between advanced and less advanced economies.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Digital Port Integration and Terminal Efficiency: Evidence from Vessel, Truck, and Container Turnaround Times in Japanese Container Terminals
Description: This paper examines efficiency at selected Japanese container terminals, and outlines recommendations for dynamic scheduling systems, operational standardization, and infrastructure optimization. Using a novel dataset that combines terminal operator records, vessel movement data, and liner service data, the analysis reveals that vessel calls, container types, and time of day significantly influence operational efficiency, and that turnaround times are primarily affected by terminal-controlled factors. The results contribute to the growing literature on port digitalization and provide evidence-based guidance for terminal operators and policymakers seeking to enhance port efficiency through digital integration.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade;Transport

Title: Digital Trade Facilitation and Container Traffic: Evidence from the Electronic Single Window Rollout in the People’s Republic of China
Description: This paper examines the rollout of the Single Window system in the People's Republic of China. It highlights that greater administrative efficiency only increases port activity when terminals have enough physical capacity to handle extra vessel traffic. The PRC's digital Single Window portal integrated customs, inspection, and quarantine functions with maritime safety administration and immigration. Analyzing its staggered rollout to ports across the country, the study finds modest overall effects on cargo volume but significant increases in port calls, with no change in average ship size, loading conditions, or processing times. The impacts are concentrated among smaller vessels and less congested terminals, indicating that administrative efficiency boosts maritime activity mainly through higher vessel frequency.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade;Transport

Title: Do Institutions Matter for Entrepreneurship?
Description: This brief highlights that reforms to institutional factors result in significant gains for the formalization of startups. It outlines policy recommendations relevant for both developing and advanced economy contexts. The brief examines how institutional factors such as business regulation, property rights, and the rule of law shape the prevalence of informal entrepreneurship. It explores how informality affects individual entrepreneurs’ growth aspirations and looks at the moderating role of gender in this relationship. It suggests that policymakers focus on improving the quality of entrepreneurship by lowering barriers to formality, strengthening institutional protections, and creating incentives for entrepreneurs to scale up within the formal sector.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: How to Assess Crisis Risks: Developing a New Early Warning System at the Asian Development Bank
Description: This report presents a new early warning system to help detect economic vulnerabilities in Asia and the Pacific. The new early warning system is powered by machine learning and a rich dataset of more than 1,500 indicators. Representing a shift toward more adaptive forecasting, it integrates traditional macrofinancial metrics with emerging risks and broadens the lens for assessing vulnerabilities. An intuitive companion dashboard delivers frequently updated forecasts, making insights accessible for policy dialogue with governments and development partners.
Subject: Economics;Finance

Title: Monthly Debt Roundup (November 2025)
Description: The ABO Monthly Debt Roundup provides a snapshot on changes in financial prices such as foreign exchange, yields, equity as well as recent monetary policy rate movements in the region. In addition, the ABO Monthly Debt Roundup provides data on foreign investor bond flows of local currency government bonds for the PRC, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines and Thailand.
Subject: Capacity development;Finance;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Buyer Market Power, Transport Mode, and Exchange Rate Shocks
Description: This paper examines how international buyers’ market power and transport mode affect exchange rate pass-through to export prices. Using customs data of Bangladeshi garment sector, the study finds that exports are concentrated among buyers and key trade hubs influencing transport choices. Large buyers use their market power and air freight reliance to buffer exchange rate shocks. Overall, buyer characteristics significantly shape exporters’ responses to external shocks, with implications for trade dynamics and regional economic resilience.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Statistical Capacity Building in the Digital Age
Description:
The growth of big data and artificial intelligence (AI) offers unprecedented opportunities to fill statistical gaps by using nontraditional data sources such as satellite images, ship signals, and mobile phone pings.
The Asian Development Bank supports this transition in developing member countries through four key areas: research and development, such as piloting AI use cases in agriculture and disaster monitoring; training; addressing resource constraints, including high-resolution imagery costs; and strengthening the core functions of domestic statistics offices for sustainable AI integration.
Subject: Capacity development;Economics;ICT;Knowledge management

Title: Geopolitical risk, capital flow volatility, and asset market spillovers
Description: [Pacific-Basin Finance Journal] Geopolitical shocks are found to heighten financial uncertainty and disrupt cross-border capital movements, with the magnitude of these effects depending on domestic structural characteristics. This paper examines the effects of country-specific geopolitical risk on capital flow volatility and asset markets across 29 emerging and advanced economies over the period 2000–2023. Using panel regressions and a panel structural VAR framework, the results show that geopolitical risk raises bond yields and leads to exchange rate depreciation, with stronger and more persistent effects in emerging economies. Asset markets for advanced economies are affected mainly through lower equity prices. The impact on capital flow volatility is slightly higher on average for advanced economies but remains more persistent for emerging economies. Greater financial development, higher central bank independence, and lower public debt mitigate the adverse effects of geopolitical risk on both capital flows and asset markets. These findings highlight the importance of strong macroeconomic fundamentals and institutional frameworks in building resilience against geopolitical shocks.
Subject: Economics;Finance

Title: Asia Bond Monitor - November 2025
Description:
Emerging East Asian Local Currency Bond Markets: A Regional Update
Emerging East Asia’s financial conditions remained broadly stable between 1 September and 31 October amid balanced risk factors, according to the Asia Bond Monitor November 2025. Reduced uncertainty following trade agreements with the United States (US), continued US monetary easing, and sound regional economic fundamentals cushioned against lingering global risks. The region recorded equity market gains, equity and bond portfolio inflows, and narrowed risk premiums, while local currencies weakened against the dollar amid US monetary policy uncertainty. Outstanding local currency bonds expanded 3.2% quarter-on-quarter (q-o-q) to reach USD29.5 trillion  at the end of September, driven by USD3.2 trillion of new issuance on 3.5% q-o-q growth. ASEAN+3’s sustainable bonds outstanding rose 3.9% q-o-q to reach USD994.4 billion, led by expansions in ASEAN markets. This edition includes analysis on (i) geopolitical risks and capital flow volatility; (ii) bond market depth and monetary policy transmission; and (iii) ESG investing and systemic banking risks.
Subject: Economics;Finance;Industry and trade

Title: Remittances, Exchange Rates, and the Role of Financial Development
Description: This paper examines how financial development can mitigate the exchange rate appreciation effects of remittances, based on a global sample of 146 economies over the period 1996 to 2021. The findings indicate that appreciation effects are most pronounced in high remittance-receiving economies, consistent with the “Dutch disease” mechanism. Stronger domestic financial markets and market-based financial deepening emerge as key channels for buffering against these effects.
Subject: Economics;Finance

Title: To Reduce Inequality, Governments Should Spend Strategically
Description: Income inequality remains high in many nations, yet evidence shows that targeted public spending can help narrow the income gap when institutions are strong and finances are well managed.
Subject: Economics;Education

Title: Beyond Pretty Pictures: Combined Single- and Multi-Image Super-Resolution for Sentinel-2 Images
Description: This paper sets out a method for increasing the resolution of satellite images by reconstructing high-frequency details. This can achieve significant performance improvement and could support scalable and cost-efficient geospatial analysis. The paper presents SEN4X, a hybrid super-resolution architecture that combines the advantages of single-image and multi-image techniques. This method enhances Sentinel-2 imagery to 2.5-meter ground sampling distance. The results confirm that super-resolved imagery can narrow the performance gap between freely available Sentinel-2 data and costly high-resolution imagery.
Subject: Economics

Title: Panama Canal Drought and Supply Chain Disruptions in Asia-US Trade: Evidence from Micro-Level Trade Shipments and Vessel Trajectory Data
Description: This paper examines the impact of the 2022–2023 Panama Canal drought on container trade between Asia and the United States. It provides insights on valuing supply delays and shaping resilient trade policies for Asia’s export-driven economies. Disruptions at global chokepoints propagate shocks through supply chains. This paper finds that the 2022–2023 drought induced significant delays in Panama Canal voyages and erased normal-time efficiency gains. Delays were largest for metals and light manufactures, while food shipments saw no significant effect.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Weekly Debt Highlights (November 2025)
Description: [November 3, 10, 17, and 24, 2025] The Weekly Debt Highlights Report provides a summary of the relevant key macroeconomic data and monetary policy updates released in the previous week in the various economies covered by ABO. The report also includes select government and corporate debt issuances, credit rating updates, and other relevant regulatory developments. The report is published every Monday.
Subject: Capacity development;Finance;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Does Digitalization Lead to Climate Awareness? A Cross-Country Panel Data Analysis
Description: This paper explores how digitalization affects climate change awareness across 65 countries from 2005 to 2020. The study applies advanced econometric methods to uncover a U-shaped pattern that shows digitalization initially has little or negative impact, but significantly boosts awareness once digital maturity is reached. Renewable energy consumption has a similar pattern. Household consumption and corruption control enhance awareness, while human development, capital formation, and trade openness show negligible effects. The findings support targeted fiscal policies to improve digital infrastructure and boost climate awareness.
Subject: Climate change;Economics;ICT

Title: Geopolitical risk, capital flow volatility, and asset market spillovers
Description: This paper explores how country-specific geopolitical risks affect capital flow volatility and asset markets across 29 advanced and emerging economies from 2000 to 2023. While these risks have negative spillovers across all economies, emerging economies are especially impacted through bond yields and exchange rates. The paper also shows that deeper financial development, higher central bank independence, and lower public debt can help mitigate the effects of geopolitical risks and strengthen resilience.
Subject: Economics;Finance

Title: Protecting Asia’s Natural Resources is Profitable
Description: [Asian Development Blog] Restoring and protecting nature pays off in profits, jobs, economic growth and increased investment.
Subject: Economics;Environment

Title: Digital Transformation and Entrepreneurial Innovation in Developing Asia: Comparative Evidence and Policy Lessons
Description: This brief investigates whether digitalization promotes entrepreneurial innovation in developing economies, focusing on Asia, and how policymakers can provide support. The brief highlights that digitalization is a powerful driver of entrepreneurial innovation across developing economies, but its impact depends on skills and institutions—not infrastructure alone. It explores the need for policies that help equip entrepreneurs to use digital tools, institutions that lower barriers to participation, and strategies that ensure benefits extend beyond larger firms and advanced hubs.
Subject: Economics;ICT;Industry and trade

Title: The Quality of Digital Entrepreneurship Systems and Economic Resilience: A Cross-Country Analysis During Covid-19
Description: [Singapore Economic Review] By enabling flexible adaptation of business models in the face of external adversity, digital technologies and infrastructures enhance the organizational resilience of entrepreneurial businesses. Their resilience, in turn, helps economies better withstand shocks and thus contributes to broader economic stability. This study explores the relationship between the quality of a country’s digital entrepreneurship systems, as measured by the Global Index of Digital Entrepreneurship Systems (GIDES), and its economic resilience during the COVID-19 pandemic. Based on a cross-country analysis of 100 economies, we find that stronger digital entrepreneurship systems are associated with smaller downward revisions in GDP forecasts, which suggests greater resilience to economic shocks. The quality of a country’s digital entrepreneurship framework conditions plays a crucial role in moderating the adverse economic impact of the pandemic, and this effect is particularly pronounced in emerging and developing economies. These findings highlight the importance of strengthening digital entrepreneurship systems to enhance economic resilience.
Subject: Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Determinants of secondary school student performance during the COVID-19 closure in Bhutan
Description: [International Journal of Educational Development] This paper uses value-added models and panel data from a comprehensive set of high-stakes secondary school exams to assess determinants of student performance during the coronavirus pandemic in Bhutan. Gender gaps, urban-rural gaps, and socioeconomic gaps do not appear to have widened substantially after the pandemic-related closure. Student characteristics (sex, household wealth, access to computer at home) and school characteristics (boarding facilities, urban location, class size, computers) predict performance for a sample of over 4000 public school students (approximately one-third of Bhutan’s entire final year cohort in 2021). Quantile regression analysis shows that home learning environment (parental education) is a predictor of Class XII performance for higher- performing Class X students. Computer ownership at home clearly improves English performance, but the pattern is less clear for Dzongkha, which has limited software and internet content for instruction. Influence of past performance and gender is stronger for students in the low-performing group. Boarding facilities played a unique role in Bhutan’s pandemic response, potentially mitigating learning losses and offsetting household differences that condition education outcomes.
Subject: Economics;Education

Title: Mechanization and Financing Can Extend Older Farmers’ Working Lives
Description: Expanding financing, easing access to machinery and adapting training can support older farmers in Asia and the Pacific and keep them productive as the workforce continues to age.
Subject: Social development and protection

Title: Asia Small and Medium-Sized Enterprise Monitor 2025: Global Uncertainty, Trade, and Private Sector Businesses in Asia and the Pacific
Description: This publication reviews the landscape of micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs) in 26 countries in Asia and the Pacific. Accompanied by a database, it aims to support policymakers in promoting MSME development and resilience.
Subject: Industry and trade;Private sector;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: How We Can Build a Cleaner, More Connected Southeast Asia
Description: The blog discusses Southeast Asia’s shift toward cleaner energy through innovative financing, regional cooperation, and technology adoption. It emphasizes transition finance, policy alignment, and social measures to ensure an inclusive and sustainable transformation.
Subject: Finance

Title: Identifying Cyclone Impacts on Fishing: A Data-Driven Toolkit for Sustainable and Resilient Fisheries
Description: [Sustainability] Tropical cyclones (TCs) can lead to significant social and economic losses, with the fisheries sector being especially vulnerable to their impacts. There is a growing need to develop new methods for impact assessment, especially as regards assessments in real time and impact forecasting. The objective of this paper was to develop an open-source, automated toolkit that can assess the impact of TCs on fishing activity by tracking changes in the number of fishing boats caused by a TC event using publicly available satellite and cyclone intensity data. The toolkit can provide retrospective analyses of how fishing activity was affected in a given country and year, and it can also nowcast/forecast likely fishing activity changes resulting from approaching or hypothetical TCs. The toolkit automates data extraction, processing, and the application of a Vector Generalized Linear Model to estimate a historical relationship between TCs and fishing activity. This relationship can then be used for nowcasting or forecasting likely TC impacts on fishing activity based on TC path, windspeed and translation speed. By providing timely, transparent, and scalable assessments of cyclone-related disruptions, the toolkit contributes to the sustainability and resilience of coastal fisheries and supports proactive risk management and informed policymaking in the face of climate-related hazards.
Subject: Climate change;Disaster risk management;Economics

Title: Turning Wealth Into Innovation: The Surprising Power of Inheritance Taxes
Description: By reducing complacency among heirs and redistributing opportunity, well-designed inheritance taxes can foster innovation, competition, and economic vitality.
Subject: Economics;Governance and public sector management

Title: Does Childcare Cost Women More? A Study of the Gender Income Gap in Pakistan
Description: This paper examines the economic costs of caregiving for women in Pakistan, revealing a significant wage penalty. Using nationally representative data from the Pakistan Social and Living Standards Measurement Survey 2019–2020, the results of various econometric models show that caregivers earn 44%–48% less than non-caregivers. Each additional child reduces wages by 12%, especially with young children. Penalties vary across wage levels and are more severe in rural areas due to lower education and job returns. The findings highlight unequal burdens on low-income and rural mothers.
Subject: Economics;Gender;Social development and protection

Title: Modernizing Data Dissemination: How Standardization Enhances Efficiency, Interoperability, and Accessibility
Description:
This policy brief highlights the importance of the Statistical Data and Metadata eXchange (SDMX) standard in transforming data dissemination.
By adopting SDMX, national statistics offices, central banks, and other data producing agencies can streamline workflows, improve data accessibility and interoperability, and align with international best practices. The brief underscores that realizing the full benefits of SDMX requires strategic planning, robust governance, technical infrastructure, and ongoing investment in skills and capacity, ultimately supporting more efficient, transparent, and user-centered data dissemination.
Subject: Economics;ICT;Knowledge management

Title: Mobile Internet Connectivity and Household Wealth in the Philippines
Description: This analysis of data from the Philippines finds that mobile internet connectivity leads to higher household wealth. The results suggest that mobile internet connectivity significantly increases household wealth, with effects that persist across education levels and are more pronounced in urban than in rural areas. Improved connectivity stimulates activities in key economic sectors that create employment opportunities. It also enhances educational outcomes and promotes female labor force participation.
Subject: Economics;ICT

Title: Quantifying the Impact of Typhoon Phanfone on Philippine Port Activity Using Automatic Identification System Data
Description: This paper explores how high frequency automatic identification system data can be leveraged to assess the near real-time impacts of typhoons on maritime activity. The analysis centers on Typhoon Phanfone, which struck the Philippines in December 2019. Using a Bayesian structural time series model to analyze daily ship traffic, the study quantifies disruptions at ports affected by typhoons. The proposed framework provides a valuable tool for rapid impact assessment, aiding disaster risk management, port operations planning, and resource allocation.
Subject: Disaster risk management;Economics;Industry and trade

Title: Public spending, private gains: the gendered impact of exogenous fiscal policy shocks
Description: This paper examines the gender-specific effects of exogenous public spending shocks across a global sample using a novel identification strategy and local projections. It finds that investment shocks reduce female labor force participation but improve wages and maternal health, while consumption shocks raise service employment and tertiary enrolment. Spending on education delays labor entry but boosts wages; health spending increases agricultural jobs but may initially raise maternal mortality; social protection stabilizes rural employment. Effects vary by income, gender inequality, and labor informality.
Subject: Economics;Gender;Governance and public sector management

Title: Monthly Debt Roundup (October 2025)
Description: The ABO Monthly Debt Roundup provides a snapshot on changes in financial prices such as foreign exchange, yields, equity as well as recent monetary policy rate movements in the region. In addition, the ABO Monthly Debt Roundup provides data on foreign investor bond flows of local currency government bonds for the PRC, Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines and Thailand.
Subject: Capacity development;Finance;Industry and trade;Regional cooperation and integration

Title: Asia-Pacific Climate Report 2025: Unlocking Nature for Development
Description: About 75% of GDP in Asia and the Pacific depends on nature, but it remains overlooked in economic planning. Healthy ecosystems support public health, livelihoods, fiscal stability, and climate resilience. Scaling nature finance requires upgraded governance, policies, and data.
Subject: Climate change;Economics;Environment;Finance

Title: The 2025 report of the Lancet Countdown on health and climate change
Description: [The Lancet] Driven by human-caused greenhouse gas emissions, climate change is increasingly claiming lives and harming people’s health worldwide. Mean annual temperatures exceeded 1.5°C above those of preindustrial times for the first time in 2024. Despite ever more urgent calls to tackle climate change, greenhouse gas emissions rose to record levels that same year. Climate change is increasingly destabilising the planetary systems and environmental conditions on which human life depends. Authored by 128 multidisciplinary experts worldwide, the 2025 report of the Lancet Countdown on health and climate change is the ninth—and most comprehensive— assessment of the links between climate change and health. The data in this report reveal that, as the health risks and impacts of climate change break concerning new records, progress is being reversed across key areas, further threatening health and survival. However, the evidence in this report also exposes important opportunities to accelerate action and prevent the most catastrophic impacts of climate change.
Subject: Climate change;Economics;Health

Title: Fighting Heat at the Workplace
Description:
Labor is one of the poor’s most valuable assets, but rising exposure to extreme heat is threatening their health and productivity. With radical changes in temperatures becoming more frequent and intense, millions of workers—especially those in outdoor and informal sectors—face conditions that are not only uncomfortable but hazardous. Without proper safeguards or infrastructure, the very asset meant to empower the poor becomes a source of vulnerability.

Findings from the Asian Development Bank’s Beating the Heat report show that the current heat exposure can reduce work capacity by 7% to 20%, depending on labor intensity, even in shaded conditions. For workers performing heavy labor in direct sunlight, productivity losses can increase by another 10%. For those whose livelihoods rely on physical labor, especially the poor, extreme heat poses both a health risk and a diminished economic opportunity.
Subject: Economics;Gender;Health;Industry and trade;Poverty;Social development and protection

Title: Public Spending and Inclusive Growth: An Empirical Analysis Across Economies
Description: This paper investigates the inclusive impact of different components of government spending in 191 economies (1980–2023). It finds that well-targeted spending can foster more inclusive growth. The findings suggest that policymakers should prioritize targeted expenditures on health, education, and social protection—areas that directly and disproportionately benefit disadvantaged people—while also strengthening governance mechanisms to ensure resources reach the intended beneficiaries. This can foster more equitable and resilient economic outcomes in both advanced and developing economies.
Subject: Economics;Governance and public sector management

Title: Asia’s Graying Workforce: Can AI be the Silver Lining?
Description:
AI and digital innovation can help extend working lives, strengthen pensions, and build more resilient systems for the aging population.
Asia and the Pacific faces a dual challenge: rapid population aging and the disruptive impact of artificial intelligence (AI). By 2050, 81 million—one in five people—will be 65 or older, straining pension systems already under pressure.

Currently, 40% of older adults lack access to any form of pension. Meanwhile, the rise of generative AI and automation could worsen pension challenges by displacing workers and reducing the pool of contributors. In East Asia and the Pacific, up to 90% of jobs could face AI displacement. This convergence of aging and technological disruption calls for urgent policy actions and better data to guide responses.
Subject: Economics;Health;Social development and protection

Title: Weekly Debt Highlights (October 2025)
Description: [October 6, 13, 20, and 27, 2025] The Weekly Debt Highlights Report provides a summary of the relevant key macroeconomic data and monetary policy updates released in the previous week in the various economies covered by ABO.
